speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 10. We're interviewing Dr. William Perl. I had asked you how you were treated by the Americans, and you started to tell me how you were treated by the American--

speaker: interviewee
text: Authority.

speaker: interviewer
text: --government and authorities. How were you treated by the American people? How were they? Were you received well by them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very well. Everybody tried to be Uncle Sam to me, to tell me how happy-- everybody gave me America. They were very nice. I was very much upset about the lack of knowledge, of understanding-- lack of understanding on the part of most American Jews. I had arrived shortly before Yom Kippur. And I went to the synagogue and through something I'm walking mid-Manhattan, and [INAUDIBLE] in a few minutes it was between [INAUDIBLE] or between the Torah readings, so there was not much going on. And they were standing around me, a Jew from Europe, and they asked questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the questions were so-- showing so much of lack of understanding. The most typical one was by one man who said, tell me, the Jews are supposed to be intelligent people. Why do they not make shalom with Hitler? Why do they not make peace with Hitler? That was such a complete not knowledge of what was going on. But most were concerned about the state of the Jews in, in Europe, they were. How did you make your living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started earning money amazingly fast. I went first to the HIAS, as everybody did, and asked what they suggested I should do. And they said, so you don't have big eyes and big expectations, we'll help you. We suggest that you try to get into the work with this [INAUDIBLE] in New Jersey. There's always demand for people to work in this field. And I said, thank you very much. And I didn't need anything from them. I had about $80, which was good enough. I always lived from one day to the other, but I was used to live on big style, you know. I had to in order to impress.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I saw in the paper, I looked through the paper and I saw in the paper an advertisement of a lecture agency. [? Lee ?] Keadick, K-E-A-D-I-C-K. So I went to him, and I told him I'm a good speaker. I was quite active in Jewish affairs in Vienna-- I didn't tell [? him, ?] I tell you now-- and was known to be a good speaker. Because I'm a good speaker, and I would like to start to give lectures here. He said, on what subject? I said, on the political situation in Europe. And he said, I am mainly specializing on the entertainment business. And this is not for you because people, when they go on vacation, they do not, they do not want to hear about Europe. And do you have something to offer in this field? I don't know whether he asked me, but I told him I have something to offer in this field possibly, too. And I had made in Europe, I started on my own, the psychology of handwriting, graphology. I had learned everything that exists. He said [INAUDIBLE] well, it's not much. We thought it was all in German. And very seriously [INAUDIBLE], and I was very much impressed by it. And I told him I could give lessons on graphology. He said, yeah, we might have something. Tell me what-- how do you [? see ?] graphology?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he thought it was a kind of fortune-telling which you see on the beaches. And I told him, it is whenever you write, you act, and you act in a very special, individual way, so individual that the banks pay on this writing on you. And your, your friends recognize you by your voice and by your appearance, by your [? dress, ?] and by your handwriting, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he-- why is this so? And I told him it is so because we do when we write, each, each, each line which we, which we make, we connect to imagined points with one stroke. And the way we connect these lines and form these strokes tells this is a problem. In a, in a [INAUDIBLE], it's a problem-- shows our attitude of problem solving.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the, the, the energetic people will connect this with an energetic stroke. The hesitating person will show, on close investigation, that he is a rather hesitating, slow-going guy. The very impatient one will make mistakes. How well you plan shows how you use the line-- whether you come to the end, or maybe you roll too far, or too-- shows your planning. And I posed it in an academic way. And he says, this just might be interesting. Show me. Analyze my handwriting. And I analyzed his handwriting. I was very good in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: He pulled somebody else, says, analyze her handwriting, his secretary. And I really could tell him. [? Said, ?] are you OK? He says, I, I'll help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he give me a, he, he gave me a recommendation to some Jew who knew Mrs. [? Losenger ?]. [? Losenger ?] was the family who owns hotels in the Catskill Mountains. And I, I got hired [INAUDIBLE]. Bought for me a tape, a projector and a screen. And I put together a lecture with, with slides, which I showed personalities who were well-known, and interpreted and showed how [INAUDIBLE] they were of Mussolini and of Hitler. But also some American person, personalities, too, of, of Abraham Lincoln, of Washington. And with this lecture, I went to, to Mrs. [? Losenger. ?] And she wasn't interested so much in the lecture, but she was interested in the handwriting. And I analyzed her writing, and she was quite stunned. And I analyzed her [? relatives ?] and she was quite stunned.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then she wanted to know about the lecture. I told her about the lectures. I give the lectures, and this was a big success. And I got her recommendation to develop some tests. And with her recommendation, I was a made man in the, in the [INAUDIBLE] circles. What attempts did you make to stay in this country during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: What decided me to stay here?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, what attempts did you make to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --allowed to stay legally in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I went to the naturalization and immigration center and told him, I want, [? I have ?] a-- I'm here and I'm here for 30 days on a temporary visa. What should I do? I want to stay here longer. He said, you can't. You can stay only 30 days unless you show that you can't go on. I said, it's very simple. I can't go on. He said, prove it to me. He wanted to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wrote to several South American countries, Brazil and Argentina, but it was impossible to get a visa. And the whole time, lawyers from Vienna, and I, my health is not very good. And I understand your country is very good for people who have some trouble with their health, and I asked for a visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, everyone-- you can't have it, and so on. And there's four or five of these letters, I think it was only three. No, four. It was four.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went to this guy again and told him, what should I do? He says, well, we have to admit you for the duration. I says, fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when the war broke out, I give lectures all over and I made really good money. I was, I was an entertainer who was very much asked for. And entertainment brings big money. I was, I was, it was to say, I had never made so much money as I made here in these few weeks. I got $100 for the lecture, and some, some, sometimes more, too. And I analyzed after the lecture, their writings, shorter writings, for $5 or for $10. It was a fortune then, $5 or $10. And well, I made out quite all right. And when Pearl Harbour came, the next day in the morning I was at the-- was not the same day, the next day after it was already announced here. The 7th or the 8th of December. I was in front, standing in line of a, a military enrollment station. It was, was the, was the Army. And the Army told me, we can't take you because you are a citizen of an enemy country. And you are too old, and now we don't call out people yet in this age. I was- it was 1940. I was 34 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I continued in my work. And in April, I, I tried to get in the Navy. I wanted to get into the military. I wanted to fight the Germans. And the Navy told me-- no, to the Navy I didn't go. I hoped. I thought, will I, will I, rise to get to be higher up and get to some sergeant or so?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote a letter, a very nice letter, which actually I have-- which if I would have known that it comes up, I would have prepared it-- which says, we are very sorry, but we can't take you. We don't take now non-citizens. But they did take residents, and I was a resident. But I would have been called if I would have been younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, I went to, to a, to a, to one of the big bosses in the Army recruiting station. He was a lieutenant colonel. I told him I want very much to get in, and I am a very capable man.

speaker: interviewee
text: I should be very valuable to your intelligence service. I speak five or six languages and I'm very familiar with the cultures there. What can I do [? that's worth ?] [? anything? ?] He says, either way, we can't draft you. But you can volunteer for earlier induction, because you are draftable as a [INAUDIBLE] earlier. So I volunteered for earlier induction. And this is a very funny experience at this station where you fill out the forms. I lived in, I lived in Manhattan in the 72nd through 94th Street section of West of Manhattan, where all the Austrians and all the German Jews lived also in [INAUDIBLE]. The Germans lived up in, in, around down to 50th Street. They call this the Fourth Reich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was there, and young people also had already been drafted at that time. And he told me, when he gave me the form, when he says, which, which foreign languages do you speak? Which languages do you speak? Don't say English. Because people thought, what-- I would have thought-- what foreign languages do I speak? English. So I gave some of the languages which I spoke. And I was drafted and was sent to the induction center at-- I don't remember the name. It was the [INAUDIBLE] Induction Center in New Jersey.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there I was, from there I was sent within three weeks, assigned through the, through the, through the quartermaster training center in Fort Lee, Virginia. And I've never been through, through the basic, basic training. As soon as I arrived, I [? told, ?] I had trouble, first of all, with the sergeant. I, I, I'm a very un-handy person. When I was supposed to make the bunk, I had trouble with it. Well, I went to the sergeant. And the sergeant was a typical southerner, professional sergeant. The sergeant was actually a corporal and from the south. Very nice, very tough, and very stupid, I think, but very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: I told him, listen, I want to know, Corporal, whether in the Army your, your, your superior of you, is he only for the training, or whether he can come to you with your-- with his personal problems, too? He said, sure, son. If you have a personal problem, this is our problem. Your problem is my problem. What is your problem?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told him, you will find me good when it comes to repeat the instruction with to learn the general orders that you have to learn by heart-- general orders. But I have suffered all my life by my un-handiness. And so he says, oh, let's [INAUDIBLE]. I said, when we get these sticks-- we didn't have rifles to train with. There were not enough rifles here. I always lose the, lose the stick. And I can't make the bunk. I try, but it never comes out. And sergeant comes in, you come in, or the PFC comes in and looks. And I was one of the last bunks. And he looks at the bunk and goes [? throws it-- ?] he throws it all into air. Air. [? He says, ?] we can help this then. He will assign somebody to make the bunk for me. And then I told him-- I didn't tell him, but I went to the, to the, to the [INAUDIBLE], to the military intelligence section. And I told them, and they shipped me-- they asked me-- they tested languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was, there was, a man there, he said, speak Italian? And there was a [? second ?] guy who was with, with me with all the European Jews speak Italian. He spoke a little bit of Italian, but he mixed it up with words which sounded Italian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Speaks French. I speak French. He spoke a few French words. He knew a little French, but German he spoke. We were both shipped to the military intelligence training center in [INAUDIBLE] [? in Maryland. ?] And yeah, after I went through the military intelligence training school for 12 weeks, which was very good, gave me very much. We could learn a lot there. Only they fed you so much that you couldn't learn it all what they gave you. It was 12 weeks from, actually, the mornings until 6:00, or 5:00 in the afternoon, with half an hour, or an hour, lunch break. And they stuffed in you, but I made out all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I was through with this, this training, I went to the chapel. And I wanted to get to the general and tell him that they could use my handwriting knowledge for military intelligence purposes. And I, I said, so how do you get a private [? tour ?] to the, to the commanding general? I didn't want to go to somebody lower. So I [? thought ?] totally separate. And the Jewish chaplain was a nobody. But I went to the [INAUDIBLE] chaplain and told him [INAUDIBLE]. He was very much impressed and he introduced me, give me a letter of introduction to the general-- General [? Banfield. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- I analyzed his writing, and his secretary's writing, executive officers. And he established for me a section handwriting with all people who might think it might be helpful-- psychologists and other people whose IQ I was given. And I, I was from both of these in a very short time, I got two stripes, a corporal. Then I got two more stripes [INAUDIBLE] with a [? T. ?] I was technical sergeant. And I was in charge of this handwriting section to train people in the POW for prisoner of war interrogation. That was the field in which I specialized. Well, when the invasion came, I asked now to go overseas. I went again to my general. I said [INAUDIBLE] to him, because I was a guy who knew him inside out, in fact. I didn't, but I knew a lot about him. And he gave me a letter for me. The other, other people, other commanding officers of my talent, it should be used for intelligence purposes. And I was, I was shipped in a short time over. I was shipped to CSDIC, Combined Services Detailed Interrogation Center, which was the highest level interrogation center for the Allied armies, for all, all the Allies sent there. That's when I had a very funny experience. I met the commanding officer, which was a Brigadier [? Kendrick, ?] a Britisher, who knew me from my [? mother, and ?] Jews from Vienna. He had been a military attache to Austria before. But he, he used it. He used it quite extensively.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism in this country before you got into the military, or during your time in the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not during the time in the military. I experienced some after that, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about after the war later. Did you see any action in terms of battles--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a very good military record. I have four battle stars. I took part in-- I got a star for the Battle of Normandy, although I came after the landing in Normandy. But the Normandy battle was still on. I have a battle star for the, for the campaign in, in, in Belgium-- in northern France and Belgium is the campaign name. I have a third battle star for the Battle of the Rhine, and a fourth battle star for the Battle of Central Europe. I was not a fighting soldier. But I went to the front lines, or near the front lines, to interrogate important prisoners right after they were captured and when they were still in the shock of capture. And I had to find out certain people who have, who is information [? was ?] only for certain purposes. And I tell you, I won't make so many jokes about the Army, that the Army sends their physician or somebody to-- as a cook. And so I was always on the spot wherever I was best used.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave me-- the first assignment I was, when the Allies approached Vienna. Can the Allies expect an uprising or any support from the local population? To what degree?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was flown over. And the Battle of Normandy was still going on. The Battle of, of Northern France and Belgium was still already on. I was for Normandy for a few days over, too. It's just not in my records. I was in the Battle of Normandy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, I had people who had just interrogated lined up and found those whom I wanted to see. I had, had my, my-- in this case, I asked all Austrians to fall out. And many fall-- fell out who had a lot of sense, because they thought they would be better treated as Germans. And then, I asked those who were Austrian who had been active in the, in the [INAUDIBLE] labor union movement, you know, the big movement-- you know, the big one-- party of Austria. And then I picked those of whom I think thought that I might get some information.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you look back on the prisoners that you interrogated--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any one, or maybe a few, that stand out in your mind as very important prisoners, or people that you were very intrigued that you start to interrogate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You see, the important ones not necessarily connected with the rank. For example, the military knew then that the Germans were building something with, with heavy water in Norway. So there could be people in Washington who were not high in rank, but were either scientists or technical personnel. I interviewed very few really high-ranking persons. I interviewed [INAUDIBLE] Dietrich, who was commander of the Sixth, Sixth Army, I think it was. It was German Army. We staged the last German attack, [SPEAKING GERMAN], in the, in the [INAUDIBLE], in Christmas, 1944. This was, part of it was the Battle of the Bulge where we-- [INAUDIBLE] [? I was ?] [INAUDIBLE] of his a few days ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you to be in a position now where you are interrogating them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was most, most gratifying. Most gratifying.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you learn about the death camps and the Einsatzgruppen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned about the first-- of course, I knew that they are being killed. I didn't know the death camps did it. I didn't know. Because I knew all the time that the deportations are the deportations for killing, not as they say, for [GERMAN], for resettlement. Because they took old and dying persons from, from the hospitals, from the old age homes. They were shipped, too. And they [? gave you ?] [? no tools or ?] nothing. So I knew, but I didn't know anything about the [? sentence. ?] I don't think I visualized much about it. Although, knew about Dachau from, from people who went to Dachau from Vienna. But I did not know details. The first detail I learned was by a prisoner of war who told me that he had served in Russia in one of those units which had buses which killed people in automobiles and vans. That's the Jews are killed this way. It was the first, first time I learned. And then, I was then-- during the Battle of the Bulge, the Germans killed-- you might remember, or you might not know-- 72 American prisoners of war, [INAUDIBLE] prisoners of war in the Battle of Malmedy, near Malmedy. And I now resolved to find [? them. ?] They will be brought to justice. And I was, at the end of the war, with intelligence work not so important anymore, in other areas, with Russia and so on, I was transferred now, at my request, to the war camps branch. And I was assigned to the Malmedy Massacre case as one of the officers. But I saw right away, this stuff, this is wrong. The Americans had American lawyers in charge. And the American lawyers told the Germans, you don't have-- I'll have to inform you that, you, if you say something which is against you, it will be held against you, and, and so on. So the Germans very simply said-- didn't speak, didn't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, I can do it better, and I was made First Assistant Chief Interrogator. Then I became the interrogator for the-- Chief Interrogator for the Malmedy Massacre case, which was a very hard position. And we brought 72 Germans to trial. They were all found guilty. 40-some, 40 of them to hang, the others, life sentences, and 10 [? upwards ?] to 10 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was afterward a big stink, because the senator from this country, a young senator with the name of McCarthy, had received from some of his German constituents information that these prisoners were, the confessions, they were all achieved by torture, and by, by beatings, and so on. So he demanded an investigation. And it was a big investigation. And being a European Jew, I was very much the, the chief interrogator. I was very much in the limelight of the whole situation. And I, I explained to them that the, the committee understood. I said, of course, I didn't approach them with beatings or-- I wanted-- I hated them, yes. I hated them. I wish every one of them would be dead. But I knew that actually I have to follow the rules.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. We have to pause to change tape. We'll be-- come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
